title: Temporal glide symmetry enforces a parity sideband selection rule in scalar bulk media
abstract: Symmetry is a powerful way to control coupling between photonic mode families. In spatially periodic structures, glide symmetry can protect band contacts and suppress stop bands. Here we show a different role for temporal glide, a spatiotemporal counterpart combining reflection with a half-period time translation. In a scalar time-modulated trilayer waveguide, temporal glide imposes an exact selection rule linking frequency conversion to transverse-mode symmetry: the parity content of every Floquet eigenstate alternates with sideband index, up to a state-dependent sign. In scattering, this means that a mode of definite parity can emit only into the opposite transverse parity at odd sidebands and into the same parity at even sidebands. We verify the rule directly in bulk Floquet eigenstates and in finite-section time-domain simulations. An incident odd waveguide mode is converted into an even frequency sideband, while all symmetry-forbidden output channels at the analysed sidebands are suppressed to numerically negligible values. Rather than acting as a temporal copy of spatial-glide band sticking, temporal glide provides a distinct symmetry principle for converting electromagnetic energy between selected modes and frequencies.
summary: The paper demonstrates that temporal glide symmetry imposes an exact parity selection rule governing how electromagnetic energy converts between sidebands in time-modulated waveguides. By comparing this 'glide' drive to a standard 'synchronous' drive, the authors prove that only specific, alternating transverse parities are allowed for emission at different frequency harmonics. This offers a new design principle for controlling light coupling in optical devices.
main_problem: The study investigates how temporal glide symmetry imposes a strict, quantifiable selection rule on energy conversion between different frequency sidebands in time-modulated photonic waveguides.
main_result: Temporal glide enforces an exact parity selection rule: the transverse parity of any Floquet eigenstate must alternate with its sideband index ($m$), which is confirmed by showing forbidden output channels are suppressed to numerical zero.
method: The work combines theoretical analysis using Floquet theory (operator algebra, eigenvector projection) with numerical validation via finite-section time-domain simulations (FDTD).
model_or_system: The system modeled is a scalar trilayer waveguide whose permittivity is modulated periodically in time. The comparison is drawn between 'synchronous' and 'time-glide' modulation protocols.
key_observables: Parity content of Floquet eigenstates, the violation metric ($V$), wrong-parity fraction per sideband ($W_m$), and spectral reorganization patterns.
important_parameters: Modulation phase delay ($\phi$), sideband index ($m$), contrast in permittivity modulation ($\Delta\epsilon_r$), and time period ($T$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to scalar relative-permittivity modulation, meaning parity mixing must originate solely from the temporal glide drive itself.
figures_summary: Figures compare spectral evolution under synchronous vs. time-glide drives, showing reorganization of coupling sectors in the latter case; other figures quantify the vanishingly small power leakage into symmetry-forbidden channels at the glide point.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical models and symmetries (synchronous vs. time-glide) to deriving the selection rule theoretically using Floquet operators, and finally verifying this rule rigorously through quantitative metrics ($V$, $W_m$) in both theoretical matching and FDTD simulations.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful example of how underlying discrete symmetries (like glide symmetry) can impose non-trivial, measurable selection rules on energy transport in engineered photonic systems, which is highly relevant for designing frequency converters or quantum light sources.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
